race.name: Giro d'Italia Ride Like a Pro USA
race.display_name: Giro d'Italia Ride Like a Pro USA
race.tagline: 85 flat miles of Italian flair in sunny Miami—pure pro vibes for amateurs.

vitals.field_size: Hundreds of recreational riders, amateurs to enthusiasts, snowbirds included
vitals.registration: Includes custom jersey, medallion, bib, expo entry, pasta dinner, post-ride lunch—pure Italian immersion.
vitals.feed_zones: 5 fully stocked SAG stops on Gran Fondo.
vitals.cutoff_time: Flexible for all levels, no strict cutoff.
route_options: Gran Fondo: 85mi, 282ft — 4 timed segments, 5 aid stations
Medio: 56mi, minimal — 3 timed segments, 3 aid stations
Piccolo: 35mi, minimal — 2 timed segments, 1 aid station
Ragazzi Fun Ride: 3 short non-competitive distances

climate.description: February in Doral delivers balmy highs of 77°F (25°C) and lows around 65°F (18°C), with scant 2 inches of rain. Expect endless sunshine perfect for ripping open roads. Minimal wind risk seals it as cycling paradise.

terrain.surface: Silky smooth asphalt loops through Miami-Dade suburbs and urban stretches. No cracks, no climbs—just relentless speed on open roads. Paved from start to finish, built for watts over heroism.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 69. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 70.

course_description.character: Silky flat loops from Doral through Miami-Dade suburbs scream pure speed. Four timed segments spice endless asphalt with KOM hunts. Minimal elevation lets watts rule—no tactical climbing games. Urban glimpses mix with open-road blasts for pro-peloton feel.
course_description.signature_challenge: Aggregate timed segments over full 85 miles—winners forged by consistent power, not one big attack. Flats amplify small gaps; strategy trumps suffering.

logistics.airport: Miami International (MIA) just minutes away—easiest access imaginable. Fly in, roll out.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Book City Place Doral hotels for expo, pickup, and pasta dinner synergy. Weekend package seals it.
logistics.food: Saturday pasta blowout fuels rides; post-ride lunch hits right. Expo village overflows Italian eats.

history.origin_story: Born from Giro's pro parcours, Ride Like a Pro hit amateurs in 2023 across 6 countries by 2025. USA debut smashed in St. Augustine January 2025 with champ Owen Shott. Relocated to Doral Miami for 2026's sunnier, Italian-flavored winter escape.
notable_moments: 2025 St. Augustine debut with Owen Shott
history.reputation: Rising star for accessible pro vibes. Charity support and Giro branding build cult following fast. Flat-fast formula hooks speed freaks.

biased_opinion.summary: This Giro-branded blast delivers pro polish on Florida's flattest canvas—85 miles of pure wattage without a single climb to cry about. Timed segments and pasta parties make it addictively fun for speed demons dodging winter. Prestige punches above its easy terrain, but don't expect suffering glory. Global expansion and Owen Shott's star power cement its buzz. Ideal for base miles with swagger.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Pin it if you crave fast flats and Italian vibes. Skip if hills are your religion. Winter essential for northerners.

final_verdict.one_liner: Blistering flats with Giro glamour—fun but forget the climber's ego.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you're a power hunter chasing winter miles or family fondo vibes. Skip if you need climbs or elite battles—too easy for masochists.
final_verdict.alternatives: Unbound Gravel (unbound-gravel) for epic adventure, Hincapie Gran Fondo (hincapie-gran-fondo) for Southeast hills, Velothon (velothon) for Euro prestige.

citations.label: Giro Ride Like a Pro USA Official Site
Top Ride: Giro d'Italia Ride Like a Pro USA - PezCycling News

videos.title: Riding Like a Pro at the Giro d'Italia in Florida 2026
videos.description:
I finally did it, my first Gran Fondo style race. The Giro d'Italia Ride Like a Pro in Doral, Florida. 54 miles, a ton of energy, and a whole new experience on the road bike in the books.
I had no idea what to expect going in, but honestly, It was an incredible time. The course, the atmosphere, the riders and everything about it just hit different. Enjoy! 

⚡️SUPPORT DWAYNE PEDALS ⚡️
Grab a copy of East To West, my book about my cross-country bike tour: https://amzn.to/3Fu4g69

INSTAGRAM | @dwaynepedals✔️
 https://www.instagram.com/dwaynepedals/

STRAVA| https://www.strava.com/athletes/2106626✔️

WEB| https://www.dwaynepedals.com✔️

HIT THAT SUBSCRIBE BUTTON & TURN ON POST NOTIFICATIONS FOR MORE CONTENT 🛎

✔️ ROAD TO 100,000 SUBSCRIBERS ✔️

videos.title: How To Complete Your First 100 Mile Bike Ride!
videos.description:
100 miles (roughly 160 kilometres if you don’t do imperial!) is an important milestone for many cyclists. It can feel daunting or difficult, whether you’re targeting a sportive, a charity challenge or just wanting to break that magic century mark on a weekend ride. Luckily for you, Hank knows a thing or two about long distance cycling, so here are his top tips to smash your first 100-mile ride!

0:00 Intro
1:08 Prepare Your Bike
1:48 Get Comfortable
2:21 Get Your Kit Right
2:43 Get Your Body Ready
3:38 Nutrition & Hydration
4:56 Know Your Route
5:37 Pacing Your Ride

The Global Cycling Network (GCN) is the largest online cycling channel in the world, bringing together a global community of road cyclists to celebrate everything that’s great about the world of cycling.

Our videos bring fans compelling daily content including expert tutorials, techniques, training, racing, cutting-edge bike tech, unparalleled behind the scenes event coverage, humour,  entertainment, and more.

Presented by ex-pro riders, GCN offers a uniquely qualified insight into the world of cycling, and most importantly it’s fuelled by our passionate and enthusiastic fans – everyone who makes up the GCN community. We also bring the latest and greatest tech to your attention, showcase the best places in the world to ride and get exclusive access to events and races.

videos.title: How To Train For A 100 Mile Bike Ride
videos.description:
For cyclists, there is something mythical about riding 100 miles. Completing a century, a ton, or 160.9km if you prefer the metric system, is a huge achievement for a cyclist and takes hard work to get there. Manon outlines her tips for training for your longest ever ride, to help you break new ground on the bike!

Chapters:
0:00 Intro
0:50 Mindset
1:21 Specific Training
2:23 Structure
3:11 Fuelling
4:43 Riding Buddies

cycling in long distances is hard if it

i see some water oh

cycling further than you ever have done
before is the perfect challenge for a
cyclist whether it's 30 miles 50 miles
or a hundred miles it's gonna leave you
with such a good sense of achievement
and the next day you're probably going
to be looking for your next challenge
already but how do you train 400 miles
do you need to be doing epic distances
on the bike each week
well in this video i'm going to give you
a few tips to help you out
[Music]
first of all your body is so much more
capable than you think 100 miles is a
long way it's a really long way but
don't doubt yourself if you put your
mind to riding 100 miles you can
absolutely do it
mindset is sometimes overlooked and
people just focus on the pedaling and
training but mindset is really important
your mind can sometimes hold you back
more than your leg scan just know that
there will be some really tough points
in the ride but you can get through them
you are so much stronger than you think
now the good news is you don't actually
have to have ridden 90 miles to be able
to ride 100 miles but don't get me wrong
it does help if you can get close to
that 100 mile marker in training as this
is going to give you so much more
confidence going into the ride and let's
be honest most of us don't have hours
and hours each day to go out and ride
our bike but to ride 100 miles you don't
actually have to train for hours and
hours each day
hit high intensity interval training is
going to be your best friend when it
comes to riding 100 miles and you're
short on time so hit high intensity
interval training is between maybe 20 30
40 minutes long and you can fit it in in
the evening or in the morning and that's
your training done for the day if you do
that two three four times a week and
then do some longer endurance ride to
help build your base fitness that is the
perfect recipe for riding 100 miles do
that for three four weeks and you will
absolutely smash that hundred miles
set yourself a training plan write down
what you're gonna do on each day and be
realistic about it if you've got a
really busy day at work and don't set
yourself a four hour ride because we're
just not gonna do it and you only really
need to train three to four days a week
because well we do all have life
commitments and other things going on
and we don't have all day every day to
train but if you do miss a date don't
give yourself a hard time about it it's
okay something is better than nothing
if you are looking for some specific
training sessions you can do and follow
along with then head over to the gcn
training channel we have a whole host of
sessions on there from 20 minute ones to
one hour long ones these are great when
you want a specific workout instead of
just riding and not following any
structure
again a hundred miles is a really long
way and fueling is gonna be extremely
important but it's a good idea to
practice and what you're going to eat
when it comes to that 100 miles so say
if you have these new energy bars or
gels that you want to try out try them
out in training to make sure they agree
with your stomach because if you try
those gels
on the ride then they don't agree with
your stomach that's going to put an end
to your 100 miles very quickly trust me
always try and aim to eat around every
13 minutes i mean i usually start off in
the first half of the ride with real
foods like briarsh with jam or nutella
and bananas work well too but i don't
actually like bananas so i skip those
then when you reach halfway and you're
really starting to suffer like i am now
you can move to the energy bars and gels
these are going to be great to give you
that pick-me-up that you need and some
even have caffeine in and they are extra
good when you need that bit of a kick
to finish that 100 miles off
do not overlook hydration either now
it's very normal just to have two
bottles and two bottle cages on your
bike and sometimes that isn't enough for
a hundred miles so make sure you've got
maybe some cash or a card on you so you
can pop into shop and fill up your water
bottles or sometimes i mean i've done in
the past i've just gone into the pub and
that's nicely for them to fill them up
with tap water and it's been fine so
just bear that in mind because if you
get dehydrated it's not going to be very
pleasant so keep on top of the hydration
riding with a friend or buddy is one
going to make the miles tick by so much
quicker two it's fun and three it's just
nice to have someone there for some
moral support when things get
a little bit tough now if you already
have friends that ride a bike then
that's great but if you don't then why
not join your local cycling club there's
loads of clubs around that you can learn
about on facebook pages
or just word of mouth and they're a
great place to meet new people that have
the same interests and hobbies as you
and you can learn so much more by riding
with other people but today
i'm solo
none of the other gcn presenters wanted
to join me on this ride and that's okay
it's okay to ride solo sometimes i quite
enjoy it maybe next time i'll have some
company
so those are some of our top tips on how
to prepare for 100 miles if you do
attempt to ride 100 miles please do drop
down in the comments how you got on we
would love to know and read about your
story so if you did enjoy this video
make sure to give it a big thumbs up and
i guess i'll see in the next one

videos.title: How To Fuel For a 100 Mile Ride
videos.description:
Getting your fuelling spot on for long rides is probably one of the best ways to instantly improve your riding. We all know the basics, but how do you get the timing and nutrition just right to ride faster and enjoy it more? In this video, Conor shares his top tips and speaks to Precision Fuel & Hydration sports nutrition founder, Andy Blow, to get his expert advice on fuelling up like a pro.

00:00 How do you fuel for a long bike ride?
00:23 How much do you need to eat per hour?
01:07 What should you eat when cycling?
1:54 How much is right for you?
03:04 Staying on top of your nutrition during your ride 
05:20 How do you hydrate on a long bike ride?
06:31 How do you eat while cycling?
08:10 What to do if you get your fuelling strategy wrong
09:57 What about fuelling with other macros besides carbs?

Useful Links:
GCN Uploader 👉 https://gcn.eu/gcnuploader

GCN WhatsApp Channel  👉 https://gcn.eu/GCNwhatsapp

What’s your preference for fuelling? What do you usually eat on long rides? 🍌

Let us know in the comments! 👇

expert reveals the best nutrition strategy for long rides
how to fuel for a long bike ride
how many carbs do you need when cycling
videos.transcript:
one of the best ways to instantly
improve your riding is to get your
fueling spot on many of us know the
basics but how do you build on that and
get things absolutely perfect so you can
ride faster than ever and also enjoy
your riding more towards the end of a
ride but in this video I'm going to
share some tips to do just that and also
H to some expert
help so I'm about to head out on my own
long ride as I discussed some of these
tips and I think my main Port of Call is
to obviously make sure my bottles are
full and my pockets are full with food I
think what you really need to think
about is how much you need per hour to
sustain yourself for the ride you're
about to be doing and really need to
think about that in terms of
carbohydrates carbohydrates they're easy
to digest they're the main fuel source
for your body at the higher intensity
levels needed for cycling and they're
also pretty Compact and easy to carry
now you want to look for about 60 to 100
20 G of carbs per hour or more in some
cases if you're heading out for a long
ride carbs are also ideal because they
provide really fast energy to the muscle
so perfect for keeping things topped up
as you're riding now we here at gcn are
really lucky to be sported by Precision
Fuel and hydration for all our rides and
big challenges and I think the advantage
to using kind of Sports Nutrition
product is that it comes in really
compact package you know the carbs
you're getting in you can kind of really
divide them up for your ride and you can
easily transport them too but I also
like to supplement that with real food
as well so bananas rice cakes Brios with
jam on Flapjacks are great as well the
main thing though is that you want to
make sure your hourly totto is spot on
you're get enough carves in to sustain
your riding but how do you know exactly
what is right for you after all we're
all different and there is a big
difference between 60 and 20 G of carbs
per hour that's almost like double the
gels so recently I caught up with
Precision Fuel and hydrations founder
Andy blow to get his take the
fundamentals of fueling are getting
enough energy in to fuel the work that
you're going to do and if you're working
pretty hard on a bike most of the time
that means taking in enough
carbohydrates you need different amounts
of carbohydrate depending on what you're
doing so generally speaking intensity
and duration of exercise are the biggest
things that dictate how much
carbohydrate you're going to need when
you're going for longer and when you're
racing or or riding harder you're going
to need more carbohydrate when you're
going for less time or you're taking it
easier your requirements per hour are
going to drop the ballpark amount of
carbohydrate you're going to need per
hour is probably going to be somewhere
between 30 and 90 G per hour that's
quite a wide range and 30 to 60 gram an
hour is going to be most appropriate for
people who are riding for an hour to two
2 to 3 hours once you're going longer
and especially if you're racing and
going hard you're going to be pushing
towards 60 over that and towards 90 G an
hour so I'm about 40 km into my ride I'm
going for about an hour 40 45 minutes
and I'm starting to get through the food
I like to bring a handlebar bag with me
pockets are full so it takes a bit of
all those items it's not overloaded now
I've already got through three of these
chews and a banana so that's around 90
100 G of carbs in total and I'm keeping
on top of things which in the past is
something I haven't been the best I've
often started my rides really full and
loaded up so I haven't quite got the
urge to eat and then I don't eat
anything on the bike 2 hours in start
smashing it start smashing the carbs and
I feel pretty stuffed not really the
best strategy I think Andy explains this
best generally speaking in terms of
pacing your carbohydrate intake out over
the length of a ride you would want to
aim to do it relatively evenly even
though you might not feel like you need
to eat a lot eat a lot of carbs in the
first hour of 6-hour ride if you wait
until you're feeling hungry or your
energy levels are dropping you're going
to be on the back foot so actually
working out the total amount and and
portioning it out quite evenly is
probably the way to go either way I'm
quite a big guy so my hourly carb intake
is rather high and also I do tend to
ride quite intensely when I am out on
the bike because trying to keep up with
my fellow GCM presenters they're always
putting me to the sword that's another
reason why I do need to up things so I
don't fall off a cliff there's orally
speaking but it's not always the case if
you're riding slower steadier you don't
need to eat quite as much so you can
reduce this hourly carb intake either
way though we all start with different
levels of glycogen stored in our muscles
before each ride glycogen is the storage
form of carbohydrate energy in the body
it's found mainly in two places one is
inside your muscles and the other is
inside your liver the glycogen in your
liver is what keeps your blood sugar
levels up and feeds your brain with
glucose because your brain only runs on
glucose which is why you feel so rubbish
when your blood sugar levels drop the
glycogen in your muscles is there to
fuel the activity that you're doing so
that's what we rely on when we're
exercising hard and you've usually got
somewhere between 60 and 120 Minutes
worth of glycogen in your muscles if
you're well stopped before you start
exercising hydration is of course a
really big factor in getting your
fueling right and we have got a real
deep dive on the topic coming out very
soon on the channel so stay tuned for
that but right now what are the real
Basics to nail your hydration strategy
here's Andy with a Lowdown to get your
hydration strategy right you've got to
focus on the amount of water and liquid
that you're going to take in in total
the amount of electrolyte you're going
to take in and then you're going to have
to tune that to how the conditions are
on the day so the hotter it is the more
you're sweating and the bigger your
sweat rate the more you're going to need
to drink as an individual and then
alongside that the more sodium you're
going to need to take in conversely on a
cooler day shorter ride your your
hydration requirement could drop
substantially so unlike fueling which is
fairly static hydration tends to move up
and down based a lot on the weather
conditions if you don't take sufficient
hydration in alongside your fueling
you'll start to find you become
dehydrated and you'll find it harder to
absorb the calories that you're eating
and you'll find it harder to process
them and actually get benefit from the
fuel you're taking in so hydration very
much goes hand inhand with fueling and
if you get one wrong you you're going to
be in trouble trying to eat all this
food on the bike can sometimes feel like
a physical challenge to keep on top of
especially as your ride progresses but
there are a few simple things you can do
to keep on top of it all one thing
that's really helped me in the past is
to try and create some sort of alarm or
alert at set intervals throughout a ride
so you don't leave things to chance well
who actually have this set up on their
head units so you can have a custom
alert which tells you every 15 minutes
every 30 minutes or whatever how you
need to eat and it really reminds you
and that's for me really beneficial cuz
especially on Long rides you get lost in
your own thoughts and time passes in
strange ways so anything you can do to
remind yourself to stick to your plan at
the start is a really good thing also I
think if you're someone who's quite
nervous about eating whilst they're
riding for whatever reason it can be
quite hard to keep on top of things this
is something actually applied to me
especially when I was racing really
nervous stressful races it's quite quite
hard take your hands off the bars get
into your pockets and eat and then for
that reason I'd often kind of P off
eating until it was too late now I think
in these scenarios especially in kind of
group rides gels are really handy
because they're quite compact contain a
lot of carbs you can eat them with one
hand quite quickly just tearing off the
top with your teeth and getting it in
putting your rubbish back in your pocket
and but there's also even bigger gels
than this that Precision actually make
you can fill it into your bottles you
can have one bottle full of gels and
even easier to consume and throughout
the ride you can just sip from that as
well as your normal water as you go but
even with the best intentions and best
strategy sometimes things just don't go
to plan and in those cases how do you
get them back on track and what happens
if you actually do too well and eat too
many carbs is that a problem if you find
yourself blowing up on a ride because
you just simply haven't eaten enough and
you've not consumed enough carbohydrate
the first thing to do is going to be to
slow down a little bit and to start
getting some more fuel in now you're
going to want to take quite a lot in but
you shouldn't overeat not in a very
short space of time because then you run
the risk of the opposite problem which
is feeling sick and Bloated but slowing
the pace and starting to make sure that
you get back on track with your plan as
quickly as you can is the priority there
are many many different reasons why
people suffer from GI distress when
they're exercising one of the most
common ones with cyclist is taking in
very strong carbohydrate drinks in large
quantities when what you actually need
is to separate your fueling from your
hydration on a hot day this can be
really bad if you Tak in lots and lots
of sickly carb drinks and what you need
is lots of water and electrolytes then
you might be at first quenching your
thirst but sooner or later you're going
to overwhelm your gut and you're going
to start to feel quite sick and Bloated
so limit the high strength carb drinks
especially on a hot day what's the end
goals of all this then what should you
expect from getting your fueling
strategy absolutely perfect it's quite a
hard thing to quantify really especially
when you're talking about feeling but I
think what I would say is if you're rid
in a group especially and you get your
feeling right and those around you don't
towards the end of a really long ride
100 mile you're really going to see the
difference everyone else is going to be
really start a tire whereas you'll be
able to push on and stamp on those
pedals which makes quite a big
difference your performance and also
your enjoyment of the ride it must be
said though that in terms of a perfect
fuing strategy I have focused on carbs
here as the number one fuel source and I
think that is true personally I've
experimented with other methods and they
haven't worked for me but I can see how
they might work for other people and if
that is the case then go for it and I
wanted to hear Andy's take on the topic
because something like a low carb high
fat approach has had some notable
successes so what's his view on the
topic there are alternative approaches
to fueling with carbohydrates and one of
them is called the low carb high fat
approach which basically means that in
your diet and especially around the
times that you ride you rely more on fat
by not taking in carbohydrate your body
has a massive store of fat even if
you're quite lean individual you have
enough fat to Power hours and hours days
and days of activity in fact the problem
with the low carb high fat approach is
that it might work for very long
duration or very low intensity events
but it doesn't really cater for racing
at high intensities because you need to
burn energy at a rate that can't be
released from fat so sometimes when
you're doing really really long
Expedition based stuff when you're going
when your only aim is to keep going at a
low intensity for a long period of time
low carb can work but it it's very rare
we see anyone using a high carbo a low
carb approach when they're racing at a
serious level must say big thanks to and
Le from Precision Fuel and hydration for
the added insight into this topic and I
really hope you found this video useful
please give it a big thumbs up if you
did I'm about to crack on with my own
long ride now keep on eating and
drinking of course but let me know in
the comments down below what's your
preference for fueling what works best
for you if You' got any questions let us
know well do our best to get back to you
right banana on the ready let's hit it

videos.title: In Pursuit - The First Epic Ride Of The Year
videos.description:
'Now I know that Spring will come again, perhaps tomorrow'. Feeling moved by the  Edwardian poet Edward Thomas, Si organised a bike ride from Bristol to the south coast in pursuit of spring. It was a similar cycle that inspired Thomas to write a moving pastoral book about the English countryside, and helped form his career as a writer and poet in the pre war era of the 20th century. Conor, Manon and James joined Si for our first epic ride of the year, and whilst not everyone was impressed with the poetry or history lessons, the sun came out and we had saw signs of spring everywhere.... 

To follow this ride with Komoot head to this link 👉 https://www.komoot.com/tour/331562750

Subscribe to GCN+ for cycling as you have never seen it before! Live racing, shows & cycling adventure films: https://gcn.eu/plus

#Cycling #GCN #RoadBike

If you enjoyed this video, make sure to give it a thumbs up and share it with your friends. 👍

quotes.text: Tomorrow's race, I know, is going to be super fast. So, my plan is to try to just stay toward the front, find riders that who seem confident in what they're doing, and just kind of try to follow their wheels as much as possible. I know there's going to be lots of surges.

It's a beautiful summerlike day. Even though it's like winter time, people are going to be traveling from all parts of the country where there is like snow and cold and some people probably I'm sure going to be from other parts of the world. So, I know it's going to be a lot of riders and it's going to be a lot of surging and lots of fast speed.

race_day_tips.text: Aim to eat around every 13 minutes during long rides. Start with real foods like brioche with jam in the first half, then move to energy bars and gels when you start suffering in the second half.

Look for 60 to 120 grams of carbs per hour for long rides. The amount varies based on intensity and duration - 30-60 grams per hour for 1-3 hour rides, pushing towards 90 grams per hour for longer or harder efforts.
Practice your fueling strategy in training. Try out any new energy bars or gels during training rides to make sure they agree with your stomach before race day.

additional_quotes.text: Always try and aim to eat around every 13 minutes. I usually start off in the first half of the ride with real foods like brioche with jam or nutella and bananas work well too.
Generally speaking in terms of pacing your carbohydrate intake out over the length of a ride you would want to aim to do it relatively evenly even though you might not feel like you need it early on.

rider_intel.search_text: The transcripts focus on general cycling training and nutrition advice rather than the specific Giro d'Italia Ride Like a Pro USA race in Doral, Florida. Riders emphasize the importance of proper fueling for long distance events, recommending 60-120 grams of carbohydrates per hour depending on intensity and duration. The advice includes eating every 13 minutes, starting with real foods like brioche with jam in the first half and switching to energy bars and gels later. Training recommendations include high intensity interval training 2-4 times per week combined with longer endurance rides. The content stresses practicing nutrition strategies during training rides to avoid stomach issues on race day. However, no specific information about the Florida race course, terrain conditions, or race-specific challenges was found in these transcripts.
